_disclaimer: ALL ITEMS IN THIS FILE ARE SYNTHETIC PLACEHOLDERS, hand-authored to exercise the LSAT Item contract. They are NOT real LSAT items and must NOT be used as study material. Real items must be sourced from official LSAC PrepTests with redistribution licensing (D-SR11). Every item carries SyntheticFlag=SYNTHETIC.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Anyone who can lead this project must be both organized and persuasive. Dana is highly persuasive. Therefore, Dana can lead this project.
items.Stem: The conclusion follows logically if which one of the following is assumed?
items.ChoiceB: Persuasiveness matters more than organization for leadership.
items.ChoiceC: Most persuasive people are also organized.
items.ChoiceD: Dana has successfully led a project in the past.
items.WhyWrongA: 'Some' does not establish that Dana is organized.
items.WhyWrongB: A ranking of traits does not supply the missing 'organized' condition.
items.WhyWrongC: 'Most' does not guarantee Dana specifically is organized.
items.WhyWrongD: Past success is out of scope and does not establish organization.
items.WhyWrongE: CORRECT. The requirement is organized AND persuasive; adding that Dana is organized makes the conclusion follow.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] The proposal will pass only if it has the mayor's support. The mayor supports any proposal that reduces the city deficit. This proposal reduces the deficit. Therefore, the proposal will pass.
items.Stem: The conclusion is properly drawn if which one of the following is assumed?
items.ChoiceA: The proposal will pass only if the mayor supports it.
items.ChoiceB: Any proposal that the mayor supports will pass.
items.ChoiceC: Reducing the deficit is the mayor's highest priority.
items.ChoiceD: The proposal faces no organized opposition on the council.
items.ChoiceE: The mayor supports only proposals that reduce the deficit.
items.WhyWrongA: This merely restates the given necessary condition; it does not make passage follow.
items.WhyWrongB: CORRECT. We can derive the mayor's support; adding 'supported -> passes' completes the chain to 'will pass.'
items.WhyWrongC: Priorities do not guarantee the proposal passes.
items.WhyWrongD: Absence of opposition is out of scope and not sufficient.
items.WhyWrongE: This reverses the deficit conditional and does not yield passage.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] The company will be profitable this year if it lands the Henderson contract. Landing that contract requires a strong proposal. The company has submitted a strong proposal. Therefore, the company will be profitable this year.
items.Stem: The conclusion follows logically if which one of the following is assumed?
items.ChoiceA: The company has never lost a contract it bid on.
items.ChoiceB: If the company submits a strong proposal, it will land the Henderson contract.
items.ChoiceC: The Henderson contract is the company's largest contract.
items.ChoiceD: A strong proposal is necessary to land the contract.
items.ChoiceE: The company will be profitable only if it lands the contract.
items.WhyWrongA: A perfect record is too strong and does not connect this proposal to this contract.
items.WhyWrongB: CORRECT. A strong proposal is only stated to be necessary; assuming it is also sufficient yields the contract, hence profitability.
items.WhyWrongC: Contract size is out of scope.
items.WhyWrongD: This restates the given necessary condition; necessity is not sufficiency.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] All of the paintings in the exhibit are by living artists. The painting 'Blue Harbor' is in the exhibit. Therefore, 'Blue Harbor' is eligible for the emerging-artist award.
items.Stem: The conclusion follows logically if which one of the following is assumed?
items.ChoiceA: Most paintings by living artists win some kind of award.
items.ChoiceB: 'Blue Harbor' is the finest painting in the exhibit.
items.ChoiceC: Only paintings by living artists were admitted to the exhibit.
items.ChoiceD: Emerging artists are usually still living.
items.ChoiceE: Any painting by a living artist is eligible for the emerging-artist award.
items.WhyWrongA: 'Most' does not guarantee this specific painting is eligible.
items.WhyWrongB: Being the finest is out of scope for eligibility.
items.WhyWrongC: This restates the admission rule and does not establish eligibility.
items.WhyWrongD: A vague tendency about emerging artists does not link living artists to eligibility.
items.WhyWrongE: CORRECT. We know 'Blue Harbor' is by a living artist; the bridge 'living artist -> eligible' makes eligibility follow.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Whenever the server load exceeds capacity, the site slows down. The site did not slow down at any point during the sale. Therefore, the new caching system worked as intended.
items.Stem: The conclusion is properly drawn if which one of the following is assumed?
items.ChoiceA: A properly working caching system keeps the site from slowing down.
items.ChoiceB: The site slows down only when the server load exceeds capacity.
items.ChoiceC: If the caching system had not worked as intended, the server load would have exceeded capacity.
items.ChoiceD: The caching system had worked correctly in earlier tests.
items.ChoiceE: The sale attracted more visitors than any previous sale.
items.WhyWrongA: This links a working system to no slowdown, the wrong direction to conclude the system worked.
items.WhyWrongB: Making the load condition necessary as well does not tie the outcome to the caching system.
items.WhyWrongC: CORRECT. Since the site did not slow, load did not exceed capacity; the bridge makes a failed caching system impossible, so it worked.
items.WhyWrongD: Past test performance does not establish that it worked during the sale.
items.WhyWrongE: Traffic volume does not connect the absence of slowdown to the caching system.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Jae will be admitted to the honors program. After all, Jae has a grade average above 3.5, and the admissions committee met this week.
items.Stem: The conclusion follows logically if which one of the following is assumed?
items.ChoiceA: Every student with a grade average above 3.5 is admitted to the honors program.
items.ChoiceB: A high grade average is the most important admission factor.
items.ChoiceC: Most students admitted to the program have averages above 3.5.
items.ChoiceD: The admissions committee meets only when it has decisions to announce.
items.ChoiceE: Jae submitted strong letters of recommendation.
items.WhyWrongA: CORRECT. The bridge 'average above 3.5 -> admitted' applied to Jae makes admission follow.
items.WhyWrongB: A ranking of factors does not establish that Jae is admitted.
items.WhyWrongC: This reverses the needed conditional and does not guarantee Jae's admission.
items.WhyWrongD: When the committee meets does not guarantee Jae's admission.
items.WhyWrongE: Recommendation letters are out of scope for this inference.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] This mushroom is safe to eat. It displays none of the warning colors that toxic mushrooms have.
items.Stem: The conclusion follows logically if which one of the following is assumed?
items.ChoiceA: This particular mushroom grows in a region that has few toxic species.
items.ChoiceB: Every toxic mushroom displays the warning colors described above.
items.ChoiceC: Mushrooms that are safe to eat never display any warning colors.
items.ChoiceD: Most, though not all, toxic mushrooms display warning colors.
items.ChoiceE: Any mushroom that lacks the warning colors displayed by toxic mushrooms is safe to eat.
items.WhyWrongA: Regional rarity does not guarantee this mushroom is safe.
items.WhyWrongB: This yields only 'not toxic,' which is not the same as 'safe to eat.'
items.WhyWrongC: This reverses the relation and does not establish that colorless mushrooms are safe.
items.WhyWrongD: 'Most' leaves room for colorless toxic mushrooms, so safety does not follow.
items.WhyWrongE: CORRECT. The bridge directly links 'lacks warning colors' to 'safe,' making the conclusion follow.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] The bridge must be closed for repairs. Any structure that fails a safety inspection must be closed for repairs, and the bridge's inspection report was submitted yesterday.
items.Stem: The conclusion is properly drawn if which one of the following is assumed?
items.ChoiceA: The bridge is the oldest structure in the city.
items.ChoiceB: The bridge failed its safety inspection.
items.ChoiceC: Repairs to the bridge will take several weeks.
items.ChoiceD: Most structures that undergo inspection fail it.
items.ChoiceE: Inspection reports are submitted only for structures that fail.
items.WhyWrongA: The bridge's age is out of scope.
items.WhyWrongB: CORRECT. Adding that the bridge failed triggers the 'failed -> closed' rule, so closure follows.
items.WhyWrongC: Repair duration does not bear on whether closure follows.
items.WhyWrongD: 'Most' does not establish that this bridge failed.
items.WhyWrongE: This suggests failure but does not state it; submission of a report is given, not failure.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Elena will win the debate tournament. She is the only competitor who has won every one of her previous matches, and the strongest debater always wins the tournament.
items.Stem: The conclusion follows logically if which one of the following is assumed?
items.ChoiceA: The competitor who has won every one of her previous matches is the strongest debater.
items.ChoiceB: Elena has trained longer than any other competitor.
items.ChoiceC: Most of the strongest debaters win all of their matches.
items.ChoiceD: The tournament is judged under the same rules as the previous matches.
items.ChoiceE: Elena has won this tournament in a previous year.
items.WhyWrongA: CORRECT. This identifies Elena as the strongest debater, and the strongest always wins, so Elena wins.
items.WhyWrongB: Training time does not establish that Elena is the strongest debater.
items.WhyWrongC: This reverses the needed link and does not make Elena the strongest.
items.WhyWrongD: Consistent rules do not connect her record to being strongest.
items.WhyWrongE: Past victories do not guarantee this year's win.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] The new wellness policy will reduce absenteeism. The policy provides every employee with a free gym membership, and reducing employees' stress lowers absenteeism.
items.Stem: The conclusion follows logically if which one of the following is assumed?
items.ChoiceA: Absenteeism is the most costly problem the company faces.
items.ChoiceB: Providing employees with free gym memberships reduces their stress.
items.ChoiceC: Most employees will actually use the free gym memberships.
items.ChoiceD: Employees value gym memberships more than they value pay raises.
items.ChoiceE: Stress is the only cause of employee absenteeism.
items.WhyWrongA: The cost ranking of problems is out of scope.
items.WhyWrongB: CORRECT. The bridge 'gym memberships -> less stress' links the policy to the stated stress-absenteeism chain.
items.WhyWrongC: Usage rates are not the stated bridge and do not by themselves complete the chain.
items.WhyWrongD: A comparison with pay raises does not connect memberships to reduced stress.
items.WhyWrongE: This overreaches; the argument needs only that reducing stress helps, not that stress is the sole cause.
